point_1.quote: Before anyone can agree, check or hand over a dish, you and the appliance must mean the same thing by the words you use.

point_2.quote: The appliance asks questions, and the two of you arrive at the words together. You are not filling in blanks somebody else chose.

point_3.quote: This book speaks of a restaurant, kitchen, dish, chef and taster. Those words are one owner's worked example. You may see a workshop or a studio instead. You may prefer blunt words with no picture in them at all.

point_4.quote: Agree on a word for each thing the rest of this book cannot do without: 1. the person who owns the place; 2. the person who runs it day to day; 3. the person who does the work; 4. the person who checks the work; 5. the machine they will all work on; and 6. one unit of work. This is a list of meanings, not a list of required answers. Your words may use this book's pictures, another set of pictures or none at all. Everything beyond these six can wait. Naming more now would settle distinctions you have not yet needed.

point_5.quote: Write the agreed words down in the kitchen where they will be used. Never write your agreement back into this book. The book ships blank on these choices because each owner must make them. It stays blank so one owner's language never becomes everybody else's law.

point_6.quote: Do not invent a second word for the same thing. Do not stretch an agreed word to cover something more convenient.
